original_abstract: While porous metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have long been recognized for their chemical versatility, their integration into electronic devices has been hindered by typically low electrical conductivity. In this work, we demonstrate that a family of conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs can serve as an effective active layer for chemiresistive sensing. By utilizing a series of structurally analogous but chemically distinct frameworks—incorporating different metal centers and organic linkers—we developed a cross-reactive sensor array capable of detecting volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Through statistical methods such as principal component analysis (PCA) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA), the array successfully differentiates between various functional group classes, including alcohols, ketones, amines, and hydrocarbons, with over 90% accuracy. Mechanistic investigations suggest that the sensing response originates from multiple, simultaneous processes, including charge transfer and hydrogen bonding, which provide a degree of orthogonality not typically found in traditional chemiresistive materials. These findings establish conductive 2D MOFs as a tunable and robust platform for the development of high-performance, multifunctional vapor sensors.
humanized_abstract: While porous metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have been recognized for their enormous potential towards exploitation of their vast chemical space, their employment in electronic devices has generally been restricted due to low conductivity, despite various efforts toward the improvement of their conductivity. Here, we demonstrate the employment of a family of rationally synthesized highly conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs as an efficient active channel in fabrication of the highly cross-reactive and chemically robust chemiresistive sensor array that comprised series of frameworks of same architecture but with distinct sites such as metal and spacers. It is observed that upon employing sensor array into statistical analysis by means of principal component analysis (PCA) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA) with training data having more than 90% accuracy for classifying their chemical species of the various VOCs, revealed sensitivity and selectivity of at least 90% with high specificity and precision of >90% towards diverse functional group classes of alcohols, ketones, amines, and hydrocarbons, thereby confirming the viability and excellent performance towards VOC detection for versatile sensing platform with various functional groups in respective classes. Mechanistic investigation indicated that their sensitivity arises because of complex mixture of electrical resistance changes, because of charge transfer as well as hydrogen bonding phenomena present there which further contribute toward orthogonality among various resistance responses as is not often the case for single type of chemical response observed in other reported chemiresistive sensor materials. Hence, our synthetic evidence thus indicates high scope of conductive 2D MOFs as tunable sensor materials of various vapor sensing functionalities and performances.
